entry.title: SAP BDC - Driving Lite Viewer Adoption: Why Your Tables Matter
entry.content:
The Lite Weight Viewer (LWV) is one of the most impactful Client Side performance improvements SAP Analytics Cloud has shipped in the last few years. No configuration is needed for the viewer, it just kicks in automatically when a story is compatible.

That "when compatible" is where most stories are losing out.

Where we are today

Looking at our telemetry across the SAC landscape, one of the top three reasons stories fall back to the full viewer is Tables that haven't been migrated to the New Table Experience. Stories with classic table widgets get redirected out of the Lite Viewer silently. No error, no warning, just slower than it could be. 

For a refresher on what the Lite Viewer is and what conditions a story needs to meet, see the initial release blog post of the Initial Release of the Lite Viewer for SAP Analytics Cloud Stories 

How to check where you stand

The Feature Adoption section in the SAP Analytics Cloud System Overview shows you, per folder, the share of story opens going through Optimized and Lite Viewer. If a folder shows a low Lite Viewer share despite being in Optimized Experience, classic tables are worth checking.

Start with your high-traffic folders. You don't need to migrate everything,  focus where it has the most impact.

Prioritizing what to migrate 

A large tenant can have hundreds of Classic stories — most of them unused. Before touching tables, it's worth separating what's actively used from what can be cleaned up. The System Overview Housekeeping function and the Feature Adoption dashboard together give you that picture. For a practical walkthrough of that approach: Efficient Migration of SAC Classic Stories: Focusing on the Essentials

Migrating to the New Table Experience

With the 2025 Q3 QRC, SAP shipped a redesigned table building experience that is also what unlocks Lite Viewer compatibility. The migration itself is done directly in story edit mode. One thing to keep in mind: blended tables from the Classic Experience don't auto-migrate and need to be rebuilt manually.

Full details on what's new and how migration works: The New Table Building Experience in SAP Analytics Cloud 

In short

  1. Check the Feature Adoption section in System Overview — look for folders with a low Lite Viewer share
  2. Prioritize: focus on actively used stories, clean up the rest
  3. For those stories: verify they're in Optimized Experience, then check for classic tables
  4. Migrate the tables to the New Table Experience
  5. The Lite Viewer picks them up automatically from that point on

entry.title: From Numbers to Narrative: Building an AI Analytics Assistant in SAP Analytics Cloud
entry.content:
Introduction

Planning reviews should focus on decisions, not data preparation. This SAC custom widget uses a live planning data binding to surface forecasts, variance drivers, AI-generated summaries, and presentation-ready outputs — helping finance teams move faster from numbers to action.

SAC Planning already provides strong planning, reporting, and AI-assisted exploration capabilities through features such as Just Ask. This widget takes a more workflow-focused approach for FP&A scenarios by combining full-year landing projections, variance analysis, executive-style narrative generation, anomaly highlighting, and one-click presentation export into a single embedded experience inside an SAC story.

 

What It Does

It is a single SAC custom widget that connects to your planning data binding and does the preparation work automatically:

  • Full-year landing: YTD actuals + remaining forecast vs budget, calculated on load
  • Variance drivers: which accounts and dimension members are most off-plan
  • Anomaly alerts: revenue and cost lines that are materially off, flagged automatically
  • AI narrative: plain-English summary of what happened and what to do about it
  • Natural language Q&A: ask questions about your data in plain English, get answers and charts
  • One-click PPT export: a presentation-ready deck built from the live widget state

 

The starting point is a standard SAC table — actuals, forecast, budget across the full P&L.

The starting point — SAC tabular data before any synthesis

The widget takes that same data binding and turns it into the views below.

 

Seven Tabs, One Data Binding

Dashboard

KPI cards for every measure — Net Profit, Gross Revenue, COGS, Margin % — showing full-year landing vs budget. Toggle between Full Year and YTD in one click. Below the cards: a P&L waterfall, geography and product group profitability charts, EV vs ICE comparison, monthly trend, and cost vs budget. An anomaly strip at the top flags anything materially off-plan before you have to look for it.

Dashboard — KPI cards and P&L waterfall

 

 

Dashboard — segment charts, monthly trend, cost structure

 

FY Landing

The most common question in any planning review: where will we finish vs budget? This tab answers it directly. Switch between accounts using the selector at the top. The headline shows the full-year landing, the gap, and an execution risk indicator (Ahead / On Track / At Risk). The trajectory chart below shows exactly when the gap opened.

FY Landing — Net Profit projection and trajectory

 

FY Landing — Gross Revenue view, 1.0B landing vs 953.5M budget

 

Drilldown

Break down any account by dimension — geography, product, segment, or quarterly view. Each row shows YTD actual, YTD budget, YTD variance, FY landing, FY gap, and a status badge (On Track / At Risk / Off Track). Variance columns are colour-coded. The worst performers show up immediately without sorting manually.

Drilldown — P&L by account with status badges and variance

 

AI Insights

One click, no setup. The widget sends your data to a language model and returns:

  • A KPI strip — four headline numbers in under ten seconds
  • Executive Summary — 2–3 sentences: where we stand, the biggest risk, the key action
  • Strategic Mix Insights — where EV vs ICE, or Domestic vs International, is diverging from the aggregate
  • Supporting Insights — three different angles on the data
  • Action Items — three short, verb-first recommendations

The writing style adapts to the audience. Set the persona in Prompt Config: CFO, Business, Executive, or Investor. Same data, different register.

AI Insights — executive summary, strategic mix, supporting insights, and action items

 

AI Command

Type a question, get an answer and a chart. The suggestion chips are generated from your actual data — if you have a Fuel_Type dimension, you get a Fuel_Type chip. You can also type freely: "Which geography is most at risk in H2?" or "Show margin by product vs budget." Use Export Slide to push any result straight into a PPT slide.

AI Command — chips, free-text input, structured response, chart

 

Prompt Config

Four settings that improve AI output without touching code: company and sector context (included in every AI call), audience persona, additional standing instructions, and a YTD month override for when auto-detection does not match your close date. A prompt preview at the bottom shows exactly what was sent to the model and what came back — useful for debugging and for audit.

Prompt Config — context, persona, YTD override, prompt preview

 

Raw Data

The full data binding in one table — every row and column the widget is working from. Export as CSV, JSON, or raw binding JSON. If a number in an insight card looks wrong, this is the first place to check.

Raw Data — full binding with CSV and JSON export

 

One-Click PPT Export

The Export PPT button builds a deck from the live widget state — no copy-pasting, no manual chart recreation. Seven slides: title, KPI summary, FY projection, dashboard charts, segment analysis, AI insight cards, and AI Command results (if you ran a query). Charts are captured from the screen at the time of export. The deck goes into the meeting without further editing.

Exported Slides

 

 

 

How It Is Built

  • Single JavaScript file registered as a SAC custom widget. ECharts for visualisations, reads the data binding directly.
  • FY projection runs in the widget finds last closed month, sums YTD actuals, adds remaining forecast.
  • Built iteratively with Claude Code: describe behaviour → generate → test on real SAC data → find edge cases → refine.

This is an experimental implementation built using SAC Custom Widget capabilities and AI services. It is not a standard SAP feature or product roadmap statement.If you are working on something similar or want to talk through an idea, drop a comment below.

entry.title: How to create an Aging Analysis report using a User selected Key Date leveraging SAC Date difference
entry.content:
Problem definition:

In the area of reporting, for example in aging Reports there is always a requirement to calculate days between 2 dates like posting date and key date or order date and delivery date etc. In SAP Analytics Cloud generally this leads to a story defined calculation of type Date Difference. This calculated Measure definition supports the following options.

  • Granularity - This is the granularity of the date difference calculation. Supported values are Year, Month, Date, Hour, seconds, minutes.
  • Time A and Time B -  These are the 2 dates of which we want to calculate the difference between. They support Current Date, Current Timestamp and Date type dimensions of the model.
  • Dimension Context - This is the dimension context under which the difference is calculated.
  • Result Aggregation - How the difference should be aggregated. You can keep it to AVERAGE or change as per requirement.

This calculation works perfectly if you want to calculate difference between 2 existing date type dimensions, or one date type dimensions and current date/time. The calculation does not support any Input control for dimensions and hence there is no option to calculate date difference between a date dimension and a user selected key date.

Technical Solution:

We will try to build a workaround solution to calculate date difference between user selected date and Date dimension which can help us create aging report with key date as selection.

In SAC stories calculation editor, date difference calculation is done by defining a calculated measure of type Date difference and below is how the setup looks like.

The only supported option for 2 dates are Current Date, Current Timestamp and Date dimension of the model. No option for any input control selection.

We want to calculate date difference between Date dimension and user selected date.

  • Define User defined key Date selection

To capture the user selection for key date we will define a Date Input control of type range and will use custom calendar. This will give us a calendar picker to select date. 

The resultant date input control can be hidden from outline view and also disconnected from any widgets by keeping the below setting for  the Input control Linked Analysis. Its just a place holder input control with no filtering affect on any Tables or widgets.

The above will help me get the below for key date selection. ( still waiting for SAC to provide calendar pickers to select dates ( From/ To and date ranges, ....topic for another day)

Now we have created a mechanism to allow users to select key date. Our next task is to pass this key date selection to our calculation.

The date difference calculation only supports dimensions of type date. Lets build up a date dimension with our user selected key date. 

SAC supports custom dimensions. The issue is custom dimensions also do not support input controls or variables. Lets workaround this problem too.

  • Define a Script variable to be used in calculated measures

Define a script variable of type integer as shown below. This variable will store the integer pattern for selected date. If user selects 15 March 2024, it should store 15042024. We will define the logic to extract this in subsequent steps.

  • Define a Text Box and assign the dynamic text binding to the selected Key date

This will help us to read the selected key date and extract the integer pattern from it. 

Output: The user selection is assigned as shown below.

  • Define a logic to extract integer date pattern from user selected date (Mar 15, 2024)

 we will use some java scripting to derive the formatted date from above. We can assign the below code to a button which will help us calculate the value and also refresh the application.

// 1. Read selected Key Date

var seldate=Text_1.getPlainText(); // Text Box value with dynamic text binding

var monthName = seldate.substr(0, 3);
var day = seldate.substr(4, 2);
var year = seldate.substr(8, 4);

// 2. Map month name to number
var monthNum = "";
if (monthName === "Jan") { monthNum = "01"; }
else if (monthName === "Feb") { monthNum = "02"; }
else if (monthName === "Mar") { monthNum = "03"; }
else if (monthName === "Apr") { monthNum = "04"; }
else if (monthName === "May") { monthNum = "05"; }
else if (monthName === "Jun") { monthNum = "06"; }
else if (monthName === "Jul") { monthNum = "07"; }
else if (monthName === "Aug") { monthNum = "08"; }
else if (monthName === "Sep") { monthNum = "09"; }
else if (monthName === "Oct") { monthNum = "10"; }
else if (monthName === "Nov") { monthNum = "11"; }
else if (monthName === "Dec") { monthNum = "12"; }

// 3. Reorder to DDMMYYYY
var formattedDate = day + monthNum + year;

console.log(seldate);
console.log(formattedDate);

SV_KeyDate=ConvertUtils.stringToInteger(formattedDate); // Script variable assignment

console.log(SV_KeyDate);

Application.refreshData(); //Trigger Refresh after assigning value to variable

 

  • Create a calculated Measure using the Script variable define earlier (SV_KeyDate).

Use @ to read the script variable

  •  Create a calculated Dimension of type Measure based dimension using the above calculated Measure

Configure to use measure values as dimension members. Keep decimal as 0.

  • Define another calculated dimension using the previous calculated dimension.

TODATE(SUBSTRING([d/"MBD_KD"].[p/ID],0,2)+"-"+SUBSTRING([d/"MBD_KD"].[p/ID],2,2)+"-"+SUBSTRING([d/"MBD_KD"].[p/ID],4,4),"dd-mm-yyyy")

So, our scripting will assign 15032024 to script variable SV_KeyDate. Calculated Measure will be assigned this value. Next our first calculated dimension will get "15032024" as member id. Next we use TODATE() ( Syntax: ToDate("date_string";"input_format") ) function to create a date member "15-03-2024" using "15032024". The above formula creates the date member in correct format.

  • Define the Final Calculated Measure for Date difference

 Now the calculation editor allows us to select the previous defined calculated dimension as one of the date selections in Date difference calculation.

 

  • Final Result

Excel calculation of Days Difference

 key date
Date15/03/202410/05/2024
01/01/202474130
04/01/202471127
06/01/202469125
07/01/202468124
10/01/202465121
03/02/20244197
05/02/20243995
08/02/20243692
11/02/20243389

Limitations: 

Currently The story does not refresh automatically on change in Key Date selections. Button interaction has been leveraged to calculate the dimensions and the measures used in the calculation and also trigger the table refresh. This part could be improved with a scripting workaround.

Summary:

This way we can achieve calculating date difference calculation between a standard date dimension value and user defined date selection. 
If you think this post has helped you, please like and share your thoughts in the comments section below. Incase of any queries please ask the same in the comments section and I would be happy to answer the same.

Thanks for reading. I hope you enjoyed the blog.

Check the SAP Analytics Cloud topic page for more up to date information https://community.sap.com/topics/cloud-analytics

follow and read other informative blog posts on SAC browse the below link

https://blogs.sap.com/tags/67838200100800006884/

 

Regards

Nikhil

entry.title: What's New on the SAP Analytics Cloud Home Screen — Q2 2026 QRC: The New Blog Card
entry.content:
The Blog Card is a new card coming to the SAP Analytics Cloud Home Screen in the Q2 2026 QRC release:

What it is:

A dedicated card that surfaces the 10 most recent SAP Analytics Cloud blog posts from the SAP Community, directly on your Home Screen. Each entry shows the title, author, publish date, and a teaser. Click any post to open the full article in a new tab.

 

What you can do:

Pin blog posts you want to keep at the top of the card for quick access.

 

You can also show or hide the card from your Home Screen at any time.

Why it matters:

No more navigating to the SAP Community separately to stay up to date. The latest SAC product news, how-tos, and community insights appear automatically, right where you start your day.

Check your Home Screen after the release and start exploring!

entry.title: SAP Analytics Cloud - You Want to Export to Excel? Not Today. 🚫📊
entry.content:
A Step-by-Step Guide to Gently (but Firmly) Stopping Your SAC Users from Living Their Best Excel Life

We've all been there. You've spent weeks building a beautiful SAP Analytics Cloud Story. The charts are crisp, the filters are elegant, and the HR data is safely governed inside SAC's secure environment.

And then... someone discovers the Export button.

This post is your survival guide. A lovingly passive-aggressive walkthrough of every trick your users will try to get your data into Excel — and exactly how to stop them. Think of it as a game. They move. You block. They move again. You block again. Eventually, everyone goes home.

Let's begin.

🎮Level 1: "I'll just export the whole story!"

Our user, let's call him Excel-Ernst, opens the SAC story, clicks File, and grins. "I'll just export this whole thing to Excel," he thinks. Easy.

Sorry, Ernst. SAC doesn't allow a full story export to Excel or CSV. The Export Story dialog only offers PDF, PowerPoint, or Google Slides. Not quite what he had in mind.

But wait — Ernst can still see the export button in the toolbar. And that's already too much. Go to View Time Toolbar Settings and disable the export option there. Do this for every access point where the export functionality can be reached. Yes, all of them.

 ✅ Block achieved. Ernst doesn't see a export functionality for the Story. He moves on.

🎮Level 2: "Fine. I'll just export this one chart."

Ernst discovers the three-dot menu on a chart widget. "More Options → Export." A friendly little dialog appears: Export Chart to CSV. He is delighted.

By default, every chart in SAC can be exported to CSV. And tables? Tables can go straight to Excel. Right there. One click.

Here's how you stop him:

Option A (the nuclear option): Disable all widget interaction via the Builder panel → View Mode → Disable Interaction. This kills export — but also kills drilling, sorting, and basically everything else. It's the "touch nothing" option. Use with caution.

Option B (the surgical strike, recommended): Go to the Styling panel → Quick Menus and uncheck Export. This removes only the export option from the widget's context menu, leaving all other interactions intact. You can even do it per widget, so a summary chart stays interactive while the detail table does not.

✅Block achieved. Ernst's export option has vanished. He squints at the screen. He tries right-clicking. Nothing. He sighs.

🎮Level 3: "What about the Data Analyzer?"

Ernst is getting creative. He found the Data Analyzer — that nifty exploration tool accessible from certain widgets. And inside the Data Analyzer? A beautiful toolbar with Export Excel, Export CSV, and Export PDF buttons, lined up like a buffet.

This one requires a two-step fix:

  1. In Story Details, turn off "Enable Data Analyzer for all supported widgets" under Data Exploration Settings.
  2. In the Table Properties of individual tables, also disable the Data Analyzer access — because the story-level toggle alone doesn't catch everything.

Yes, both. We know.

 ✅ Block achieved. The Data Analyzer access from a Story is gone. Ernst feels personally attacked. He opens a new browser tab.

🎮Level 4: "I'll open it directly in the Excel Add-In."

Ernst has done his research. He discovered that with the right table settings, there's an option in the widget menu: "Open in Excel Add-In." He activates it. He is unstoppable.

First: make sure this option is not enabled in the table properties. It has to be explicitly activated, so as long as nobody turns it on, it won't appear.

But here's the uncomfortable truth — even if you lock down every story, every widget, and every export button: if a user has access to the underlying SAC or Datasphere model, they can still use the Excel Add-In independently to query that model directly. The Add-In doesn't create a new authorization layer. It simply respects existing SAC and Datasphere permissions.

So if the story access is based on a Datasphere analytical model, the user technically also has Add-In access to that model. "Story access yes, Excel access no" is not a supported configuration.

What you can do:

  • Remove Space Files permissions from consumer roles in Datasphere by creating a custom Role. This prevents users from actively browsing and selecting models in the SAC Excel Add In or the Data Analyzer in SAC, effectively disabling self-service Excel analysis — while still allowing them to consume SAC stories normally.
  • Centrally control Add-In deployment via Microsoft 365 administration. The Add-In should not be available to everyone — only to users with a documented business need for Excel-based SAC analysis.

✅Partial block achieved. Ernst can no longer pick models freely in the SAC Excel Add In. He mutters something about governance being "a bit much."

🏁The Boss Level: Design

Here's the dirty secret that all of the above controls are working around: if employee-level detail records are visible in a table, the data is already exposed. Export buttons or not, a determined user can copy-paste. Screenshot. Memorize (okay, probably not that last one).

The strongest control isn't a setting. It's design.

Design Principle What it means

Aggregated KPIs onlyShow sums, averages, counts — not individual records
Summary firstFor example HR dashboards show overviews; detail is the exception
Detail by exceptionIndividual-level data only in separate, access-controlled stories
Disable Data Analyzer by defaultDon't expose ad-hoc exploration unless explicitly needed

If sensitive data isn't in the story, it can't be exported from the story. Simple, elegant, and no amount of right-clicking will change it.

Final Words

Ernst is still out there. He's resourceful, well-intentioned, and genuinely just trying to do his job. The goal isn't to make his life impossible — it's to make accidental data leakage impossible, while still giving him what he legitimately needs.

Use the controls. Design defensively. And maybe send Ernst a nicely formatted PDF instead.

He'll be fine.

Have questions about SAC governance, role design, or Datasphere model permissions? Drop them in the comments below!

AI Usage Disclosure:
Generative AI was used to support language refinement and structuring of the article.
All technical content, validation, screenshots, and implementation details were created and verified by the author.

entry.title: Asymmetric Reporting Layout
entry.content:
What is Asymmetric Reporting?

It is our pleasure to announce the introduction of a new innovation in SAP Analytics Cloud, delivered as part of the 2026 Q2 QRC release.

Asymmetric Reporting introduces a new way to design tables in SAP Analytics Cloud, enabling greater flexibility in how data is structured and presented within a single table.

Instead of relying on a strictly uniform layout, where all rows and columns follow the same dimensions, hierarchies, and granularity, Asymmetric Reporting enables selected aspects of a table layout such as measures, time ranges, and visible hierarchy levels, to vary across different sections of the same table.

In practice, this means that a single table can combine multiple perspectives. For example, one part of the table may focus on actuals at a detailed monthly level, while another section presents forecasts or budgets at a higher level of aggregation. These views coexist within the same table, without requiring separate reporting objects or duplicated structures.

This capability helps users design more expressive and context-aware tables directly in SAP Analytics Cloud, better reflecting the way business questions are actually asked and answered.

 

Why it matters: Real-world reporting is not symmetric

Traditional table structures in BI and planning tools are typically symmetric by design. This means that the same dimensions, hierarchies, and filters are applied consistently across all rows and columns of a report.

While this approach ensures simplicity and consistency, it often does not reflect the reality of business reporting, especially in financial planning and analysis scenarios.

In practice, different parts of the same report frequently require different levels of detail or different analytical perspectives. For example:

  • Actuals are often analyzed at a monthly or even daily level of granularity
  • Forecasts may start from a specific cut-off date
  • Budgets are frequently reviewed at a quarterly or yearly level

Bringing all of these perspectives into a single symmetric table often leads to compromises: duplicated reports, complex workarounds, or loss of clarity in how data is presented.

From a planning perspective, this limitation becomes even more visible. An additional challenge comes from “unbooked” combinations that must remain visible for data entry and forecast collection, even when no transactional data exists yet. Business users want to compare actuals, forecasts, and budgets side by side, but each of these elements naturally follows a different logic and time structure. Forcing them into a single rigid model can reduce both usability and analytical accuracy.

Asymmetric Reporting in SAP Analytics Cloud addresses this challenge by allowing different parts of a table to reflect different structures and levels of detail, while still being part of a single, coherent model. This makes it possible to align table design more closely with real business processes, rather than adapting business logic to technical constraints.

 

Example and typical use cases

A typical example is a rolling forecast table.

In this scenario:

  • Accounts are displayed in the rows
  • Actuals are shown from January up to a selected cut-off date
  • Rest of Year Forecast starts after the cut-off date through the end of the period
  • Full Year Actuals plus Forecast provides a consolidated yearly view
  • Full Year Budget is displayed at a quarterly level
  • A Delta highlights the difference between Budget and Actuals plus Forecast

Each column represents a different perspective, with its own logic and level of detail. This is what makes the table asymmetric.

This approach is especially useful for:

  • Rolling forecasts
  • Budget vs Actual vs Forecast analysis
  • Financial statements such as P&L or Balance Sheet
  • Management reporting dashboards

 

See it in action

To see how this comes together in practice, watch the following demo:

 

In this example, notice how:

  • Time ranges adjust dynamically
  • Different measures display different levels of granularity
  • The table adapts based on user input

 

How it works: The building blocks behind Asymmetric Reporting

Asymmetric Reporting is enabled by a combination of three key innovations that work together.

First, Measure Input Control in Restricted Measures and Cross Calculations allows input controls, such as date, version, or measure selection, to dynamically drive calculations within the table.

Second, Dynamic Time Filters for “Rest of Period” automatically adjust time ranges based on the current context, enabling scenarios like “Actuals up to a selected date” and “Forecast for the remaining period.”

Third, Visibility Filters for Table Widgets control which dimension members or hierarchy levels are displayed for each measure, allowing different parts of the table to present different views.

Combined, these capabilities enable each column or row to behave independently while remaining part of a single, consistent table.

With these building blocks, you can:

  • Combine multiple reporting perspectives in a single table
  • Mix different levels of granularity (monthly, quarterly, yearly)
  • Dynamically adapt time ranges and calculations
  • Reduce the need for multiple tables or manual workarounds
  • Create reports that better reflect real business logic

 

Share your feedback

Asymmetric Reporting introduces a more flexible way to design tables in SAP Analytics Cloud.

By combining dynamic calculations, adaptive time filtering, and flexible display control, it enables reporting structures that better align with real-world business needs. This results in more intuitive reports, improved analysis, and a more efficient BI and Planning experience.

That being said, we now want to hear from you. Your feedback directly helps us improve both the product and its documentation.

Please let us know your thoughts. Your input is highly appreciated and will actively influence future enhancements to SAP Analytics Cloud.

entry.title: Building a “Plug-and-Play” Navigation Bar in SAP Analytics Cloud
entry.content:
TL; DR: Composite widget in SAC lets us combine widgets, styling, and scripting in a single widget that can be used in multiple stories. This blog walks through the development of a navigational bar composite widget where text, menu hierarchy and icons are fully configurable and centrally controlled. They are controlled either through a function in the composite widget that contains all the parameters required to render the widget or by passing the parameters from the host story using arguments. These arguments are interpreted at runtime and renders the widget accordingly.

 

1.    What is a composite in SAC?

If you have experience in developing few reports/ dashboards, you have likely repeated the same UI element or functions in multiple stories. Eg, header, navigation menu, filter panels, custom menus, footers, page initialization functions, etc.

Composites are meant to solve this problem by letting us build reusable elements of story and maintain them centrally. This helps reduce development and maintenance effort and makes standardization easier.

While the theory makes sense, how many times have we had to tinker with the font size, layout, adjust text boxes, button, etc. after copy pasting a perfectly designed widget into a new story because the requirements were slightly different.

 

2.    How can we make composites even more reusable?

We already have many ways to change the composite widget behaviour through functionalities like interface properties and passing arguments through functions.

Interface Properties and Functions

But can we push even further before crossing into the “custom widget” territory?

 

3.    A Centrally Controlled Navigation Bar

3.1  Goal

Our goal here is to develop a flexible navigational bar that can be reused across stories. It should be easily configured by either by-

  1. Duplicating the composite and changing its configuration like text, icons, menu hierarchy from a single place (like a function)
  2. Or passing the configuration through an argument from the host story

Then at runtime, composite should use the menu configuration and render itself accordingly.

3.2  Composite widget features

  • Enable seamless navigation by opening stories directly from the menu bar
  • Support expanding and collapsing the navigation bar to switch between full menu text and icon only views
  • Centralized configuration of menu labels, icons, and menu hierarchy
  • Ability to expand and collapse child menu items from their parent menu
  • Automatically expand the relevant parent menu and highlight the currently active story
  • Allow menu styling and visual behavior to be controlled via CSS

 Collapsed NavigationExpanded NavigationParameter Script Object

 

3.3  Composite widget design

3.3.1    UI structure

 

             

 

 

3.3.2    Define and initialize script variables

Initialize arrays for buttons, texts, and panels into script variables of the composite widget. Since elements are referenced by their index in various functions, we need to ensure that the order of the objects inside the arrays matches UI and “Outline”. Note that the order in the “Outline” is in descending.

 

Maintain the “Selection” type array that will store the various menu properties and its structure, in this case it is “sv_buttonConfig.”

 

3.3.3    Functions and Script Objects

Write script objects and functions for rendering the widget at run time and for various actions like parent button toggle, opening a story on menu button click, highlighting the current story button, etc.

 

 

3.3.4    Control button styling using CSS

 

 

 

4.    What Next?

Some of the functionalities that can be added:

  1. Multi-level menu hierarchy
  2. Theme switching
  3. Role based dynamic menu
  4. Passing current story filters to a new story

 

5.    Conclusion

Composite widgets are quite interesting and opens up new avenues to explore standardization. This blog is just a simple example of how far one can go with changing composite widget behaviour from one story to another. Instead of looking at composite widget as repeatable object, we can start viewing it as configurable building block.

entry.title: はじめてのSAP Analytics Cloud BI - エクスポート
entry.content:
本ブログシリーズはSAP Analytics CloudのBI機能をつかってダッシュボードやレポートの作成を行う方を対象とし、SAP Analytics CloudのBIにおける基本の操作やTipsをハンズオン形式でご紹介します。
 
本ブログでは、「作成したストーリーをPDFやPowerPointとして保存・共有したい」「テーブルやチャートに表示されているデータをExcelやCSVで取り出したい」といった場面で役立つ、ストーリーのエクスポート機能についてご紹介します。

 

 

目次

  • エクスポート機能の概要
  • エクスポート機能の利用方法 [ハンズオン]
    • ストーリーをPDF/PowerPoint/Google スライドとしてエクスポート
    • チャートやテーブルのデータをCSV/Excel/PDFとしてエクスポート
 
※ベースとなるサンプルストーリーの作成手順は、はじめてのSAP Analytics Cloud BI -サンプルモデル／ストーリーの作成- SAP Community でご紹介しています。
 
※ SAP Analytics Cloudは、四半期に１度のペースで製品のバージョンアップが実施されます。そのため今後のバージョンアップにより、本ブログで紹介する画面キャプチャや、操作が異なる可能性があることをご了承ください。（本ブログは2026年5月に作成しています。）
 

エクスポート機能の概要

SAP Analytics Cloudのストーリーのエクスポート機能を使うと、作成したストーリーや、ストーリー上のテーブル・チャートに表示されているデータをさまざまな形式でファイルとして出力することができます。
  • ストーリー全体のエクスポート：ストーリーのページをそのまま視覚的に出力します。会議資料や報告書として活用する際に便利です。
  • テーブル・チャートのデータエクスポート：ストーリー上のウィジェットが表示している集計データを数値データとして取り出します。オフライン分析や他システムへのデータ連携に活用できます。
出力可能な形式は以下の通りです。
 
 
 
 
 
 
詳しくはHelp Portalもご参照ください：ストーリーをファイルとしてエクスポート / ストーリーからのチャートデータとテーブルデータのエクスポート

 

エクスポート機能の利用方法

0. はじめてのSAP Analytics Cloud BI - サンプルモデル／ストーリーの作成 - SAP Communityで作成したストーリーを表示モードで開こう！

 

1. ストーリーをPDF/PowerPoint/Googleスライドとしてエクスポートしよう！

ストーリーの内容をそのままファイルとして保存することで、共有やプレゼンテーション用に利用することができます。
1.1. ストーリー上部のメニューバーから「ファイル」をクリックし、「エクスポート」を選択します。

 

 

1.2. エクスポートダイアログが表示されます。ファイルタイプでPDFまたはPowerPointまたはGoogle スライドを選択し、ファイル名や出力範囲などの設定を必要に応じて調整します。

ストーリー URL、所有者、モデル名、ストーリー変数、ストーリーフィルタ、ウィジェットフィルタなどのストーリー情報、および (任意で) エクスポート時間を出力ファイルに含めるには、「付録」にチェックをつけます。

 
 
1.3. 「エクスポート」をクリックするとファイルのダウンロードが開始されます。今回はPDFでエクスポートします。

 

 

 1.4. ダウンロードしたファイルを開き、ストーリーがPDFとしてエクスポートされていることを確認します。

 

 

2. チャートやテーブルのデータをCSV/Excel/PDFとしてエクスポートしよう！

ストーリー上のチャートやテーブルのデータをエクスポートすることでオフラインでの分析や他システムへのアップロード用データとして利用できます。
2.1. データをエクスポートしたいテーブルやチャートをクリックし、メニューから「エクスポート」をクリックします。
（チャートの場合はメニューから「その他のオプション」→「エクスポート」をクリック）

 

 
 
2.2. エクスポートダイアログが表示されます。ファイルタイプでPDFまたはPowerPointまたはGoogle スライドを選択し、ファイル名や出力範囲などの設定を必要に応じて調整します。（今回はテーブルデータをエクスポートします。）
  • スコープ：テーブルに表示されている内容のみをエクスポートする「視点」、表示されている内容に関係なくすべてのデータ （折りたたまれている階層や非表示になっている列) をエクスポートする「すべて」、からエクスポート範囲を選択。
  • 書式設定を含める：モデルおよびストーリーで定義されたスケール、単位、および通貨の書式設定を含めてエクスポートする場合にチェックをつけます。
  • 付録：ストーリー URL、所有者、モデル名、ストーリー変数、ストーリーフィルタ、ウィジェットフィルタなどのストーリー情報、および (任意で) エクスポート時間を出力ファイルに含めるには、チェックをつけます。
 
 
2.3. 「エクスポート」をクリックするとファイルのダウンロードが開始されます。今回はCSVでエクスポートします。

 

 

2.4. ダウンロードしたファイルを開き、データがCSVとしてエクスポートされていることを確認します。

 

 

 
今回はストーリーのエクスポート機能についてご紹介しました。SAP Analytics Cloudには他にも様々な表現や便利な機能があり、本ブログシリーズでご紹介しています。はじめてのSAP Analytics Cloud BI - シリーズまとめ - SAP Community からご参照ください。

entry.title: Create a Custom SAP BDC Data Product share it with Enterprise Databricks, extend it and send it back
entry.content:
Overview:

This blog demonstrates how to leverage SAP Business Data Catalog (BDC) in conjunction with Enterprise Databricks to create custom data products, extend them with advanced analytics and transformations, and synchronize the refined data back to SAP BDC. This integrated approach enables organizations to maximize data value while maintaining governance and traceability across their data infrastructure.

Main steps at a glance

1. Check the prerequisites

a. Enable your SAP BDC Core in SAP4Me portal, it important to provision it in the same hyperscaler and region of your Enterprise Databricks or in the future your GCP or Microsoft Fabric tenants, because you may incur some egress fees when you share data products through a BDC Connect instance. You can refer to this blog Introductory Guide to Provisioning of SAP Business Data Cloud

b. Provision your SAP Datasphere tenat in SAP BDC through SAP4Me you can refer to this blog Provisioning of Business Data Cloud : SAP Datasphere  when you provision at least allocate 4,150 CU because you need to activate object store in you tenant, so you need to increase the storage to 384 GB and memory to 128 GB and in the object store you need to add 1 block for storage, 1 block for memory and 1 block for requests you can use the SAP Business Data Cloud Capacity Unit Estimator to configure your business case

c. Create one HANA Data Lake Files space in you Datasphere tenant, and add it to an scope role to provide access

d. Provision your SAP BDC Connect instance in SAP4Me portal, you can check this blog How to provision SAP BDC Connect for Databricks?

e. Prepare your source system with all the configuration needed to connect with you SAP Datasphere tenant, in this document you can check all the connection types and prerequisites -> SAP Datasphere Connection Types

f. If you want to connect an S4HANA system then you need to configure your cloud connector for S4HANA PCE or S4HANA On-Premise connection with SAP Datasphere:

  • Step 1: Setup SAP Cloud Connector connect to SAPDatasphere
  • Step 2: Setup mapping (RFC connection) in Cloud Connector for a SAP S/4HANA On-Premise connection
  • Step 3: Create SAP S/4HANA On-Premise Connection for Data Flow and Replication Flow
  • Step 4: Replication Flow for CDS views from S/4HANA On-Premise to SAP Datasphere

2. Create the formation

a. Create your formation in SAP4Me portal. Go to Portfolio & Products -> My Product Packages tab-> Business Data Cloud product package -> Customer Landscape tab -> Formations sub-tab and choose Create Formation, enter a unique formation name and choose Integration with SAP Business Data Cloud

b. Include the following mandatory systems: SAP Business Data Cloud, SAP Datasphere and SAP BDC Connect instance. It's not mandatory to include your source system in the formation

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

3. Create custom managed data product

a. Open your SAP Datasphere tenant. First of all replicate your data. You can replicate directly to object store creating a connection from your source system to SAP Datasphere in the HANA Data Lake Files space or you can replicate an existing table coming from another space in your Datasphere tenant. If you want to use a view or analytic model you must materialize the view or model in a table, then replicate into a table in your HANA Data Lake Files space. My table is TL_ADW_OBSESSION

b. Create a formation profile in your SAP Datasphere tenant. Go to Data Sharing Cockpit -> My Data Provider Profile -> Create Data Provider Profile. In this example my data provider profile is ONB_Demo

 

c. Go to Data Sharing Cockpit -> My Data Products -> Click on Create Product (+ icon)

d. In Artifact Space select the object store space for your data products

e. Select your table in my case is TL_ADW_OBSESSION and click on Save Changes

f. Click on Switch Status and select List. When the data product is Listed please open it

g. Open your SAP Business Data Cloud Cockpit and go to Catalog & Marketplace, click on Search and we look for ONB_DEMO data product, open it and click on Share

h. Click on Add Target. Select your BDC Connect instance and click on Share

4. Extend and push it back to SAP BDC

a. In our SAP Datasphere tenant we select the space which can install data products. Go to System -> Configuration -> Business Data Products

b. Select your BDC Connect Instance and in the left side click on Add to select the space where you want to install data products

c. Open your Enterprise Databricks workspace and go to Catalog -> At the top of the Catalog pane, click the gear icon and select Delta Sharing

d. Click on your BDC Connect provider

e. Click on Mount to Catalog

f. Provide the catalog name and click on create

                                 

 

 

 

 

g. Go back to your Catalog and in Delta Shares Received you can see bdc_deltashare catalog with the table coming from Datasphere TL_ADW_OBSESSION

h. Extend the table with advanced analytics and/or transformations. Then new table in Databricks is cluster_final, the next step is create a share object. Go to Catalog -> At the top of the Catalog pane, click the gear icon and select Delta Sharing. Click on Shared by me tab and click Share data

i. Create a share page, enter the share Name and add an optional comment. Click on Save and continue

j. On the Add data assets tab, select the data assets you want to share. In this case I want to share the view cluster_final. Then click on Save and continue

k. If is the first time you share assets maybe the system will ask to grant some required permissions, click on Confirm

l. In the next step select the notebooks you want to share. In this case I don't want it. Click on Save and continue

m. In the final step select your BDC Connect provider as recipient. Then click on Share data

n. Now we have a share bdc_connect with this we are sharing the view cluster_final

o. Then please go to Catalog -> At the top of the Catalog pane, click the gear icon and select Delta Sharing. Click on Shared by me tab, click Recipients and select the recipient

p. Click on Grant share. On the Grant share dialog, start typing the share name or click the drop-down menu to select the shares you want to grant, finally click on Grant

q. Finally you need to publishing the share as a Data Product to SAP Business Data Cloud. The next image is an example about the last step to publish, please follow this documentation -> Publishing the Delta Share as a Data Product to SAP Business Data Cloud.  for the complete process and steps

r. Then we come back to SAP BDC Cockpit and we should be able to see our new data product coming from Enterprise Databricks

Summary:

In this blog, we explore how to create a custom data product in SAP Datasphere, the steps to share it with an SAP BDC Connect instance, extend it in Databrick by creating a new cluster table and finally, send it back to SAP Business Data Cloud as a new data product.

entry.title: Table Widget Improvements in SAP Analytics Cloud 2026 Q2 QRC
entry.content:
The table as the workhorse of analytics

In SAP Analytics Cloud, the table widget remains one of the most used and most relied-upon objects in any story. It is where numbers are compared, decisions are justified, and analysis is communicated.

With this 2026 Q2 QRC release, we are introducing two new capabilities that extend what tables can do. The first addresses a long-standing limitation in cross-system reporting: the ability to display complete, reliable totals in tables that combine data from multiple models. The second gives analytics developers a new degree of programmatic control over how hierarchy data is presented to end users at runtime.

Both improvements are independent. Both solve real problems. Together, they reflect a continued investment in making the table widget more capable, more flexible, and more trustworthy.

 

One table, multiple models, complete totals

Many organizations run their analytics across more than one data source. Actuals may come from one system, budgets from another. A single report often needs to bring these together in one place.

Until now, this created a specific and frustrating gap: when a table in SAP Analytics Cloud combined data from blended models, totals were not available. The aggregated row - the one that gives a report its summary, its bottom line - was simply missing. Users had to work around it, accept incomplete views, or maintain separate reports.

This capability closes that gap. Tables and charts built on blended data models can now display totals. These totals update dynamically as filters are applied, so the summary row always reflects the current view of the data.

In practice, this means a financial dashboard that pulls actuals from one system and budget data from another can now show a consolidated total row, just as any single-model table would. The report is complete. The numbers add up. The trust is there.

This is especially relevant for financial reporting, executive dashboards, and any scenario where business decisions depend on seeing the full picture across data sources - not just the detail rows.

What this capability delivers

  • Display totals in blended tables and charts
  • Totals update dynamically as filters are applied
  • Supports SAP S/4HANA and SAP BW/4HANA blended scenarios
  • Supports subquery measures and accounts with base aggregation: SUM, MIN, MAX, NO NULL, NO ZERO
  • Tooltip guidance when a measure is not eligible for totals

Limitations

This feature requires Optimized Story Designer with the new table build experience. Backend version dependencies apply: SAP S/4HANA QRC3 2025 (EPM 2025.28) or later; SAP BW/4HANA minimum 7.50 SP33.

 

Hierarchy display, on your terms

For business users

Hierarchical data is common in planning and reporting. Organizational structures, account hierarchies, cost center trees - these are the backbone of many analytical models. But not every use case calls for the full hierarchy to be visible.

Sometimes, the right view is the full tree: all levels, all members, full context. Other times, only the leaf members matter - the actionable line items, the individual accounts, the specific cost centers where data is entered or reviewed.

In the past, this choice was made at design time and stayed fixed. A table showed what it was configured to show. Changing the display required going back into the story designer.

With this improvement, that choice can now be made at runtime. Analytics designers can build stories where the hierarchy display adapts dynamically - switching between the full hierarchy and a leaves-only view based on user interaction or application logic. The end user experiences a cleaner, more focused table when they need it, without losing access to the full structure when context requires it.

For analytics developers

This capability is delivered as an extension of the existing setHierarchy() scripting function. A new optional parameter - HierarchyMode.LeavesOnly - has been added, which toggles the “Show Only Leaves” mode for a hierarchical dimension on a table widget at runtime.

The parameter is optional and backward-compatible. Existing scripts using setHierarchy() are unaffected.

A typical implementation: a planning application where users switch between an input view - showing only leaf members for data entry - and a review view - showing the full hierarchy for aggregation and context. A single script call handles the transition. No duplication of widgets, no workarounds, no loss of planning integrity.

This is currently supported on UQM table widgets. It is delivered as part of setHierarchy() and does not extend to other widget types such as charts or geomaps.

What this capability delivers

  • New optional parameter HierarchyMode.LeavesOnly added to the existing setHierarchy() scripting function
  • Toggles “Show Only Leaves” mode for a hierarchical dimension on a table widget at runtime
  • Fully backward-compatible - existing scripts using setHierarchy() are unaffected
  • Provides a clean, non-destructive way to surface only actionable leaf members, while keeping the hierarchy structure intact for writeback and aggregation

Limitations

Supported on UQM table widgets only. Not available for ListTable, Chart, Geomap, or other widget types. Live BW not supported.

 

Share your feedback

These two improvements represent targeted additions to one of the most central objects in SAP Analytics Cloud. We hope they address real needs you have encountered in your own reporting and development work.

That said, we want to hear from you. How are you using blended models today? What hierarchy display challenges have you run into in planning applications? Your feedback directly shapes how we continue to evolve the table widget.

Please share your thoughts in the comments. Your input is highly appreciated and will actively influence future enhancements to SAP Analytics Cloud.

entry.title: New SDK Capabilities: Filter by Range and Filter by Condition for SAC Custom Widgets
entry.content:
New SDK Capabilities: Filter by Range and Filter by Condition for SAC Custom Widgets

Custom widgets can now offer the same rich filtering experience as native SAC charts — Filter by Range for date dimensions, and Filter by Condition with operators like Contains, Starts With, and Between.

What's New

The SAP Analytics Cloud Custom Widget SDK has been extended with two new filter types: Filter by Range and Filter by Condition. Previously, custom widgets could only filter by member selection — choosing specific values from a list. With these additions, widget builders can now offer the same filtering experience as native SAC charts.

What This Means for Widget Builders

These new filter types are surfaced through the standard SAC Builder panel — the same panel where dimensions and measures are bound. Widget builders do not need to implement any custom filter UI. The filter dialog is provided by SAC and the filtered data is passed to the widget through the existing onCustomWidgetAfterUpdate lifecycle callback, exactly as member filters are today.

This means any existing custom widget that already responds correctly to data updates will automatically benefit from Range and Condition filters without any code changes. The filtered result set is what the widget receives — the filtering logic itself is handled by SAC.

If you have questions about these new SDK capabilities or want to share what you have built — drop a comment below or reach out directly.

 

Further reading

  • Building a Production-Ready SAC Custom Widget with Claude AI
  • SAP Analytics Cloud Custom Widget – Hands-On Guide

entry.title: Datasphere: To Slash or to Backslash? Resolving the Compound Characteristic Crisis
entry.content:
We’ve all been there. You are building the perfect SAP analytics landscape. S/4HANA, BW, Datasphere and SAP Analytics Cloud are dancing together in perfect harmony. Your master data is clean. Your compound characteristics - like Controlling Area and Cost Center - are mapped beautifully.

And then, you look at the screen. In one report, your compound key looks like 1000/2000. In the next, it’s 1000\2000.

Welcome to the ultimate separator showdown: The Slash vs. The Backslash. It sounds like a minor detail, but as any data architect knows, this is the kind of formatting chaos that drives business users crazy.

Let’s unpack why SAP Datasphere is currently giving us mixed signals.

The Standard Content Confusion

When you dive into SAP Datasphere standard content, the way compound characteristics are represented depends entirely on how you look at them.

  • The Flat Representation: If you look at a flat list or preview, Datasphere defaults to a backslash ( \ )
  • The Hierarchy Representation: The moment you switch to a hierarchy representation in the standard content, it magically changes to a forward slash ( / )

"No problem," you might think, "I will just change it!"

And you can! If you manually intervene and edit the view settings, you can force the hierarchy representation to use a backslash, too.

In the views behind the Cost Center dimension the behavior for hierarchy representation is customized via calculated columns. (Please note that this example is based on the standard dimension view for Cost Center of the Business Content "Finance Foundation for SAP S/4HANA and SAP S/4HANA Cloud")

So, should the standard dimension be adjusted? Typically, I would say "No!". But then, a native Datasphere feature enters the room to mess with your formatting again.

The "Unassigned" Node Trap

Datasphere has a built-in feature to handle unassigned nodes in hierarchies. To use it, you create a view that automatically derives an unassigned node. The catch? This derived node strictly uses the backslash ( \ ) representation.

If you are using the standard content hierarchy (which defaults to a forward slash) along with this standard unassigned node feature, your hierarchy will now display a wild mix of both slashes. Some nodes will split with " / ", while the unassigned parts split with " \ ".

 

It’s a formatting identity crisis out of the box. Of course, you could manually adjust the SQLScript view that was generated by the standard function and change the hardcoded backslash to a forward slash.
 
 

But that brings us to the ultimate question: What is actually right, and what is wrong?

The Frontend Nightmare: SAC and Excel

If Datasphere lived in a vacuum, we could just enforce the backslash. But it doesn’t.

Classic BW and CDS queries have historically used the forward slash for compound characteristics. SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC), acting as the strategic frontend, connects to all of these.

If you are a "perfect" SAP customer utilizing the full stack - Datasphere, S/4HANA and BW - SAC is going to serve your end-users a chaotic cocktail of slashes. 

This raises an even bigger architectural question: What is the right representation for SAC Public Dimensions? Since they don't support compounding natively, we are forced to actively choose and build a workaround between the slash and backslash.

And let’s not even talk about the power users who export this data to Excel. To a spreadsheet, a backslash can change how formulas react, how text-to-columns works or simply cause visual confusion.

The root of all evil here? The generic use of the backslash in Datasphere.

How Should We Deal With This?

  • The "Hope for the Future" Strategy: Keep the forward slash for the hierarchy representation and hope that the Datasphere product team aligns with historical SAP standards in future updates. The downside: Flat representations on a Datasphere Analytic Model will still show a mismatched backslash.
  • The "Forced Consistency" Strategy: Manually change the standard hierarchy views to use the backslash everywhere. The downside: You are modifying standard content and fighting against the historical forward-slash standard of BW and S/4HANA.
  • The "Anti-Compounding" Strategy: Simply stop using compounding in Datasphere altogether and manually enforce the forward slash across your custom models.

Dear reader, I would like to hear from you:

  • Which of these architectural strategies are you using in your live projects?
  • Have you found a cleaner workaround?
  • Do you have an idea, why Datasphere relies on the backslash for compounding?

Help Fix the Slash Mess!

We shouldn't have to build custom workarounds for standard features just to keep our slashes straight. I have created an SAP Customer Influence Request to get this fixed.

Let's convince the Datasphere product team that the forward slash deserves to rule them all (or at least, that consistency should):

SUPPORT ME!

entry.title: From Blueprint to Reality: SAP BDC Data Architecture Deep Dive Miniseries- June 2026
entry.content:
The SAP Business Data Cloud Data Architecture Deep Dive Miniseries is a focused, four-episode live session program running from June 1-5, 2026, designed exclusively for SAP Partners.

Each session is 1 hour, delivered online, and packed with practical guidance, demos, and strategic insights. Whether you’re just getting started or looking to sharpen your technical edge, this miniseries has something for every experience level.

Register Now

To utilize this opportunity to build your SAP BDC architecture expertise. Sessions are available in multiple timezones.

Date

LSC Registration Link (or)

Alternative link (PE)

01-Jun-26

Data Architect curriculum and SAP Architecture Center

PSD_WEB_00010833

02-Jun-26

Unpacking the Knowledge Core for Data Architects

PSD_WEB_00010852

03-Jun-26

SAP BW and HANA Modernization as a Data Architect

PSD_WEB_00010862

05-Jun-26

Leveraging the Open Data Ecosystem To Drive Success

PSD_WEB_00010869

 

Episode 1- June 1, 2026: Data Architect curriculum and SAP Architecture Center 

Kick off the series with an introduction to the SAP Architecture Center, a powerful resource hub now live and with SAP Business Data Cloud at its core.

In this opening session:

  • What the SAP Architecture Center offers — solution reference architectures, diagrams, scenarios, component lists, and use case examples
  • How SAP Partners can leverage it for top SAP BDC use cases, including Enterprise Data & Analytics Strategy, SAP BW Modernization, SAP Databricks integration, and AI for SAP BDC
  • How to integrate these resources into your Enterprise Architect toolchain (draw.io and more)
  • How SAP Partners can actively contribute to the Architecture Center

Episode 2- June 2, 2026: Data Architect Miniseries - Unpacking the Knowledge Core for Data Architects 

Dive deep into the technical and strategic architecture of SAP Business Data Cloud in this expert session tailored for solution architects.

Key topics covered:

  • Introduction to SAP BDC and its role in unifying SAP and non-SAP data — structured and unstructured — with built-in governance, analytics, and AI
  •  Data products as reusable, governed assets and a strategic approach to data management
  • Technical architecture highlights: zero-copy sharing, object store persistence, and SAP Databricks integration
  • Live demo of an SAP BDC-powered intelligent application
  • Overview of the Data Product Catalog, metadata management, and lifecycle features
  • How SAP Partners can add unique value and best practices

Episode 3- June 3, 2026: Data Architect Miniseries - SAP BW and HANA Modernization as a Data Architect

SAP BW modernization is one of the most critical journeys for many organizations today. This session provides solution architects with clear pathways and practical guidance for modernizing SAP BW and integrating with SAP BDC.

Key topics include:

  • The strategic value of SAP BW modernization - simplified architecture, greater agility, and readiness for AI-driven use cases
  • A comprehensive overview of SAP BW Modernization pathways
  • Common use case patterns where SAP BW queries and models are transformed into agile SAP BDC data products, enabling advanced dashboards and AI/ML capabilities
  • Details on the Free SAP BW to SAP BDC Migration Assessment — a tailored migration road map for your landscape
  • How to add unique value and best practices as an SAP Partner

Episode 4- June 5, 2026: Leveraging the Open Data Ecosystem To Drive Success 

Close out the miniseries with a strategic session focused on architecture options and transition planning for moving to SAP Business Data Cloud — enabling unified data operations, faster innovation cycles, and insight-driven decision-making.

Key topics include:

  •  Transition Scenario Benefits and value realization frameworks
  •  SAP HANA Cloud + SAP BDC — bi-directional data exchange to power hybrid innovation
  •  Greenfield or Migration — flexible paths for both fresh starts and phased modernization
  • Seamless connection of data products with SAP Analytics Cloud, SAP Databricks, and more
  • SAP’s complimentary assessments and services to help you plan and move confidently
  • Strategic roadmap and practical steps for a smooth, successful transition

Who Should Attend?

This miniseries is designed for: Architects | Consultants | Presales | Project Managers | Data Scientists | Data Analysts | Administrators

Direct links are available in this blog or Visit SAP Learning or Partner Portal to find and register for each session.

 

✨Surprise:  

Attend all four episodes of this miniseries and earn an exclusive digital badge 🏆 

#SAPBusinessDataCloud #SAPArchitecture #DataArchitecture #SAPPartner #SAPBDC #SAPBWModernization #DataProducts #SAPDatabricks #SAPAnalytics #CloudData #EnterpriseArchitecture

entry.title: SAP Analytics Cloud Dimension Tables Move to the File Repository
entry.content:
Introduction

Dimension tables in SAP Analytics Cloud are moving to the file repository. This completes the journey that started with stories, models, data actions, multi actions and currency conversion tables — and it brings with it a significantly simpler approach to privilege management, a cleaner user experience, and a unified way to organize all your planning artifacts in one place.

In this post we walk through what is changing, what the new experience looks like, and what you need to know about the central migration that will move your existing dimensions automatically. This enhancement is shipped with the QRC3.2026 upgrade on QRC and preview systems and with wave 2026.11 on fast-track tenants.

 

Why This Matters: Key Benefits

All in one place

Dimension tables are the final object type to make the move. With this release, stories, models, data actions, currency conversions, and dimension tables all live in the file repository and follow the same handling paradigm. There is no longer a separate mental model for dimensions — everything works the same way.

Greatly Simplified Privilege Maintenance

Managing access to dimensions has historically been one of the more painful aspects of SAP Analytics Cloud administration. The new approach eliminates that friction in several important ways.

  • There is no longer any need to maintain roles specifically to hide a dimension that contains sensitive data. File repository sharing handles this naturally.
  • When a new dimension is created in "My Files", safe defaults apply automatically: only the creator has full control, plus administrators with access to private user folders. No one else can see the new dimension until it is explicitly shared.
  • You can configure access on a folder once and then simply move dimensions into that folder — they automatically inherit the folder's privileges. This is a significant time saver for tenants with many dimensions and complex access requirements.
  • A long-standing pain point is also resolved: the role maintenance UI used to display every individual dimension, including hundreds or thousands of "Date" and "Version" dimensions. This made it nearly impossible to identify such specific dimensions and, in tenants with large numbers of dimensions, caused the UI to become slow due to missing lazy loading. Individual dimensions no longer appear in the role maintenance UI at all.

Promote private dimension tables to public dimension tables

You can now convert a private dimension table by saving it in the file repository as a public dimension table to benefit from folder-level access management, re-usability and access to data management capabilities.. 

 

What Changes in the User Interface

Public Dimensions in the File Repository

Public dimensions are now visible in the file repository browser alongside models, stories, and other artefacts. You can rename them, move them, update their descriptions, or transfer ownership — all without affecting any models that reference them.

Sharing Instead of Role Maintenance

To change privileges for a dimension or grant access to other users or teams, you now use the standard file repository sharing functionality. Individual dimensions no longer appear in the role maintenance UI.

Public Dimension Tables Landing Page

The public dimension table landing page no longer loads and displays all public dimensions on open. Instead, it shows the 25 most recently used public dimension tables — consistent with the behavior already in place for models and currency conversions.

Saving a New Public Dimension

When saving a new public dimension, the file repository save dialog appears, allowing you to specify the parent folder, the name, and a description — exactly as you would when saving any other file repository artifact.

Save as Public Dimension

Convert a private dimension table to a public dimension table to benefit from the previously mentioned benefits of folder-level access management, re-usability and access to data management capabilities.

Creating Dimensions Inside a Model

It is no longer possible to choose "Make Public" when adding a new dimension directly inside a model. The alternatives are:

  • Create public dimensions upfront before creating the model and reference them when building the model.
  • Create a new private dimension, save the model, then use "Save As Public Dimension" to create a copy and "Dimension Table > Replace" to swap the Private dimension for the newly saved public one.

New Create Dimension Dialog

The "Create Dimension" dialog has been redesigned. It now includes a file repository browser for selecting existing public dimensions of a given type — Account, Organization, or Generic. In seamless planning models, it only allows picking dimensions saved in the same SAP Datasphere space as the model.

Replace Dimension Dialog

It now includes the same file repository browser for selecting existing public dimensions.

Private Dimension Tables

Private dimension tables do not appear in the file repository browser. However, access management has moved away from roles and uses file repository functionality:

  • You now control who can see and edit a private dimension using the same file repository sharing mechanisms available for all other artifacts, reachable directly from the model or dimension editor. Sharing a model with a user or team automatically shares all private dimensions of that model with the same privileges.
  • It is also possible to restrict access to individual private dimensions further than the model itself.

 

Central Migration: What Happens to Your Existing Dimension Tables

The migration to the file repository runs automatically as part of the QRC3.2026 upgrade on QRC and preview tenants or as part of the wave 2026.11 upgrade on fast-track tenants. Here is what you need to know.

What Gets Migrated

  • All public dimension tables receive a file repository artefact.
  • These artefacts are created in a new folder: Public > Dimension Tables.
  • Private dimensions are technically migrated as well but they will not be visible in the file repository.

What does not change

Note that the migration does not change the dimension tables itself. IDs and content stay the same. No effects on the consumption in models, stories, data actions etc. are to be expected.

How Existing Role Assignments Are Handled

The migration applies the same algorithm used for earlier file repository migrations such as Data Actions, Multi Actions and Currency Conversions.

  • Teams following the naming convention "DimensionRole_<role name>" are generated for roles where at least two users have that role directly assigned. Those users are added to the generated team, and the relevant dimensions are shared to that team. For roles with single user or only team(s), the permissions will be attached to the individual or team(s).
  • If the tenant already has all users organized in teams with roles assigned to those teams, no additional teams will be generated — existing teams are reused.

Privilege gaps after migration

Due to the way file repository privileges work, some users may find they are missing access to dimensions after the migration.

  • Before the migration, a create-only role was sufficient — users automatically had full control over any dimension they created as the owner. The file repository does not work this way.
  • To achieve the same level of control as before, users need a role that explicitly grants Read, Delete, Update, and Maintain privileges.
  • We recommend reviewing your role assignments after the migration and updating them accordingly.

Import of Transport Packages

When importing transport packages created before the migration, dimension tables are automatically saved/migrated to the file repository.

Bookmarks to Public Dimension Tables

Browser bookmarks created to directly access public dimension tables will cease to work after the migration. We need to add a prefix for all public dimension tables to avoid name collisions with other objects.

Behavior During the Migration Window

While the migration is running, creating and deleting dimensions is blocked and will result in backend errors. Depending on the number of dimensions and roles in your tenant, this could take several hours.

The migration runs right after the upgrade of your tenant to QRC3/wave 2026.11. This means users can be logged in while the migration is in progress and may encounter these backend errors during that window.

 

Summary

The move of dimension tables to the file repository is a significant step forward. It completes the unification of all object types under a single, consistent artefact management model, eliminates long-standing pain points in role maintenance, and gives administrators a much more intuitive and scalable way to control access to sensitive data. We recommend reviewing your role assignments after the migration and updating them accordingly.

entry.title: Improved Data Access in Stories: Introducing the Data Panel and Drag & Drop Creation of Charts
entry.content:
The BDC SAP Analytics Cloud Team is constantly looking for ways to make data storytelling faster, more intuitive, and more accessible. With the 2026 Q2 QRC, we are excited to introduce a new foundation and set of improvements that fundamentally rethink how you discover, access, and work with data in SAP Analytics Cloud Stories.

The Data Panel: Your Starting Point for Self-Service Analytics

The Data Panel is a brand-new feature that builds on top of our Available Object List concept, which was introduced with the Optimized Experience. While the Available Object List was a first step, we recognized that it did not fulfill its potential as it always required you to select an existing object and launch via the Builder Panel. With this in mind, we've made significant improvements, not only to decouple the experience, but to meaningfully improve overall usability.

Transparent Data Access

In the past, consumers found it difficult to figure out how to add a data model to a Story. Most of the time, consumers would add an existing object which limited them to existing models or datasets, without realizing there was an additional entry point in the toolbar that provided access to data sources or to upload a file.

We are proud to announce that friction is now gone. Immediately when you create a new story, the Data Panel is surfaced right at the forefront, improving self-service workflows from the very first moment.

The Data Panel makes it immediately clear that you can add data from:

  • An Existing Model or Dataset
  • A Data Source
  • Upload a File

 

Recents & Favourites in Model Selection: Find Your Data Faster

We've recognized that consumers who consistently build insights from the same set of data models are frustrated by having to search for the same model again and again.

Now, once you select an existing model or dataset, you have access to:

  • Recents: data models you have actively used in the past, surfaced immediately so you can reuse them with a single click
  • Favourites: models you've explicitly starred for quick access

This dramatically reduces the number of clicks required to introduce a new data model into your story, especially for consumers who work repeatedly with the same datasets. Rather than browsing through folder structures or relying on search, your most relevant models are always a click away.

ⓘ it is an improvement that is available across SAP Analytics Cloud, such as Open a Theme, Save As a Story, and so on

 

Central Overview of Data

Once a data model has been added, the Data Panel becomes your central reference point for understanding and working with your data. Rather than navigating away from your Story, you can explore the full structure of the model directly within the panel:

  • Primary Structure (Account or Measure as it depends on your model)
  • Secondary Structure (Measure or Structure as it depends on your model)
  • Dimensions
  • Location Dimensions

This makes it significantly easier for consumers to understand the overall structure of their data model as they build additional visualizations, all without leaving the story canvas.

 

Continuous Exploration Across Data Models

It was very important to also enable continuous exploration. Consumers can now add additional data models directly within the Data Panel, and as each new model is added it is surfaced immediately, making it easier to navigate across multiple data models within the same story.

For complex dashboards, the Data Panel goes even further. As a consumer selects a specific visualization on the canvas, the Data Panel dynamically updates to reflect the data model associated with that visualization. If the consumer clicks away, the panel retains the last used data model, ready for the next visualization to be created.

For Blended Visualizations, selecting the visualization will invoke the full 'All Data' workflow, clearly highlighting via a blue indicator which models are in use, so consumers always have clarity on the data sources driving their analysis.

More Usability Improvements!

We also addressed a number of usability requests that customers have raised in the past:

  • Toggle between Description, ID, and ID & Description in the Data Panel making it easy to switch how entities are displayed 
  • Resize the Data Panel to suit your screen and content needs
  • Open in Modeller or Data Analyzer: under the three-dot menu, consumers can now open the current data model in a new tab, either in the Modeller or the Data Analyzer for further exploration. 

Decoupled from the Builder Panel

One of the most significant changes is the decoupling of the Data Panel from the Builder Panel. In the past, the Available Object List was always invoked through the Builder Panel.

Now, a lightweight toolbar on the right has been introduced, making it easier to:

  • Access or collapse the Data Panel independently
  • Access the Styling Panel or Builder Panel directly, without having to first open the Designer Panel
  • Collapse or Expand all right panels at once with a single action

This separation means the Data Panel can stand on its own as the primary workspace for data-first workflows, with the Builder Panel available as a manual configuration tool rather than the default starting point.

Powerful Search with Number of Entities Preview

For stories with large or complex data models, finding the right entity quickly is essential. As soon as a consumer invokes search within the Data Panel, a preview of matching entities is shown in real time, something that was not possible before.

For more complex data models, such as Universal Account Models with Multiple Hierarchies, the search experience goes even further. Matching entities are shown not only in the main panel list, but also within the account hierarchy dropdown menus, ensuring consumers can locate the exact field they need regardless of where it sits in the model structure.

Drag & Drop for Charts: Build Visualizations Naturally

With the Data Panel now surfaced alongside your story canvas, we've also improved drag and drop chart creation. The combination of the two makes this capability significantly more powerful.

Consumers now have two supported drag and drop workflows:

  • Drag from the Data Panel into the Builder Panel: the existing workflow, still fully supported
  • Drag from the Data Panel directly onto a Chart Widget on the canvas

Because the Data Panel is now decoupled, consumers can collapse the Builder Panel entirely and continue to build and modify charts purely through drag and drop, reducing panel clutter and keeping the focus on the canvas.

Updating Existing Visualizations

Previously, changing the data model on an existing visualization required the consumer to first clear the data source manually, which was a multi-click process. Now, consumers can simply drag a field from a different data model in the Data Panel directly onto an existing chart or into the Builder Panel, and the visualization will dynamically update to the new data model.

Where relevant, a confirmation prompt will appear to let the consumer know that the drag and drop action will cause a reset of the current chart configuration, keeping consumers informed while still dramatically reducing the number of steps required.

 

Building the Foundation for Data-First Workflows

With the introduction of the Data Panel, we've built the foundation for more advanced self-service use cases. The goal is to reduce the dependency on the Builder Panel as the primary interface, and instead establish a data-first workflow where consumers start with their data, explore its structure, and build visualizations naturally. The Builder Panel remains available for manual configuration, but it is no longer the gatekeeping step it once was.

These improvements, the Data Panel, Recents & Favourites, and enhanced Drag & Drop, are not isolated changes. Together they represent a deliberate shift toward a more intuitive, flexible, and powerful authoring experience in SAP Analytics Cloud Stories.

We're looking forward to hearing your feedback as you explore these features in the 2026 Q2 QRC. Keep a lookout on the Roadmap Explorer to see when we extend this to Tables and allow Drag & Drop directly to the canvas!

entry.title: How To Trigger Automated Email Notifications Based on KPI Thresholds in SAP Analytics Cloud
entry.content:
Introduction

In many business scenarios, dashboards are expected to do more than just display KPIs. Business users also expect proactive alerts whenever critical numbers cross a certain threshold so that immediate action can be taken.

Recently, I worked on a requirement in SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) where users needed to receive automatic notifications whenever a KPI exceeded a predefined limit. The alert had to be sent both as  an SAC notification and as an email. Another important requirement was that the process should run automatically on a specific day of the month without users having to open the dashboard manually.

In this blog, I’ll walk through the approach that was implemented using SAC scripting APIs such as getResultSet() and sendNotification() along with scheduled story publication.

Business Requirement

The requirement was straightforward but very useful from a business monitoring perspective.

The business wanted to:

  •             Monitor a KPI available in an SAC dashboard
  •             Compare the KPI value against a defined threshold
