  •             Trigger an alert whenever the threshold condition is met
  •             Send email notifications to a predefined group of users
  •             Deliver alerts through: SAC notifications ,  Email notifications       
  •             Automate the entire process so that alerts are triggered on a scheduled date even if  nobody opens the dashboard

For example, if a KPI value becomes greater than 1000, users should automatically receive an alert.

Solution Overview

The solution was implemented using SAC Application Designer / Optimized Story scripting capabilities.

The main logic was written inside the On Initialization event of the story. Whenever the story initializes, the script reads the KPI value directly from the chart using the getResultSet() API.

The retrieved value is then converted into a numeric format and compared against the threshold value. If the condition is satisfied, the sendNotification() API triggers both SAC notifications and email alerts for the configured users.

To automate the process, the story publication was scheduled monthly to developers ID. During the scheduled execution, the story initializes automatically, which in turn executes the notification logic without requiring manual dashboard access.

High-Level Process Flow

The overall process works as follows:

  1.           The SAC story/dashboard initializes
  2.           The On Initialization script gets triggered
  3.           The script reads the KPI value from the chart using getResultSet()
  4.           The KPI value is converted into numeric format
  5.           The value is validated against the threshold
  6.           If the condition is met, a notification is prepared
  7.           SAC notification and email are sent to the configured users
  8.           Story publication scheduling automates the execution on the required date

During scheduled execution, the script runs automatically and sends alerts if needed.

Prerequisite

Before implementing this solution, ensure that email notifications are enabled in SAP Analytics Cloud.

 

Technical Implementation

1. Fetching KPI Value from Chart

The KPI value can be accessed using the getResultSet() API available on charts and tables.

 Below is the logic used to fetch the KPI value:

 var check_data = Chart_1.getDataSource().getResultSet()[0][Alias.MeasureDimension].rawValue;       This retrieves the raw KPI value from the chart result set.

2. Formatting the KPI Value

The returned value may sometimes be in string format, so it is important to convert it into a numeric value before comparison.

var value = Number.parseInt(check_data);

3. Threshold Validation

Once the value is converted, it can be validated using a simple conditional statement.

var notificationContent = ""; 
var title = ""; 
if(value > 1000){ 
    title = "Alert - The value is greater than 1000"; 
    notificationContent = "Dear User, Please be informed that the value is    greater than 1000"; 
} 
else{ 
    title = "Key figures look good";
    notificationContent = "Key figures look good"; 
}

4.  Sending Notifications

The sendNotification() API is used to send alerts to specific SAC users.

Application.sendNotification(
     {
        title: title,
        content: notificationContent,
        receivers: ["user_id"], 
        mode: ApplicationMode.Present,
        isSendEmail: true 
     });

This sends:

  •             An SAC notification
  •             An email notification (if email settings are enabled)

Complete Script

var check_data = Chart_1.getDataSource().getResultSet()[0][Alias.MeasureDimension].rawValue;
 console.log(check_data); 
var value = Number.parseInt(check_data); 
var notificationContent = ""; 
var title = ""; 
if(value > 1000){
  title = "Alert - The value is greater than 1000"; 
  notificationContent = "Dear User, Please be informed that the value is greater than 1000"; 
} 
else{
  title = "Key figures look good";
  notificationContent = "Key figures look good"; 
}
 Application.sendNotification(
  { 
    title: title,
    content: notificationContent,
    receivers: ["user_id"],
    mode: ApplicationMode.Present,
    isSendEmail: true 
  });

Automating Alerts Using Scheduled Publication

Initially, notifications were getting triggered only when users opened the dashboard manually.

However, the requirement was to run the alerting process automatically on a specific day every month.

To achieve this, the SAC story publication feature was used.

The story was scheduled for publication to a specific user on the required date. During the scheduled execution, the story automatically initialized in the background, which triggered the On Initialization script.

As a result:

  •             KPI validation happened automatically
  •             Threshold conditions were checked
  •             Notifications and emails were sent automatically whenever the condition was met

This approach helped in building a simple but effective automated KPI monitoring mechanism inside SAP Analytics Cloud without requiring any external integration or custom scheduling tools.

OUTPUT EMAIL :

Final Thoughts

This solution is very useful for proactive business monitoring scenarios where users need immediate visibility into KPI fluctuations.

Using SAC scripting APIs along with scheduled publication makes it possible to build lightweight alerting mechanisms directly within SAP Analytics Cloud.

Some additional enhancements that can be explored:

  •             Dynamic threshold configuration
  •             Multiple KPI monitoring
  •             Role-based recipient management
  •             Integration with external notification channels
  •             Exception dashboards for alert tracking

I hope this blog helps anyone looking to implement automated KPI-based alerts in SAC.

If you are here, thank you so much for taking out your time to read this. Your feedback will be highly appreciated.

Thanks,
Himanshu

entry.title: Live Versions for Planning Models in SAP Analytics Cloud: Microsoft Azure Is Here!
entry.content:
Introduction

The live data access story for SAP Analytics Cloud planning models keeps getting stronger. When we introduced live versions in QRC4 2025, we opened the door for planning models to connect directly to live SQL sources without replication. Google BigQuery was the first supported source, followed by Snowflake in QRC1 2026. With this release, Microsoft Azure joins the portfolio — and the pattern should by now be a familiar one.

If you missed the earlier posts in this series, here is a quick recap: live versions allow a planning model in SAP Analytics Cloud to reference fact data directly from an external source at query time, without importing or replicating that data into SAC. The live data is read-only — you cannot write back to the source — but you can use it in calculations, data actions, and stories, and copy it into planning versions when needed. This applies to SAC models that are not deployed to SAP Datasphere. For seamless planning scenarios, live connectivity to SAP Datasphere is the way to go. Learn more:

  • Live Versions from an SQL Source for SAP Analytics Cloud Planning
  • SAP Analytics Cloud Planning with Live Snowflake Data
  • Unlocking the Next Chapter of Seamless Planning in SAP Business Data Cloud with Live Versions

With Microsoft Azure now supported, customers running their data on Azure can bring that data directly into their SAC planning models without any replication pipeline. Let's walk through how it works.

 

How Does It Work?

There are two ways to get started with live versions from Microsoft Azure: you can create a new model directly from your Azure data, or add a live version to an existing model. We will cover both approaches.

Creating a New Model from Azure Data

Alternatively, you can create a planning model directly from your Azure data source — a clean approach when starting from scratch. The video example is based on Snowflake. It is the same workflow, just choose Azure instead.

  • Go to the Modeler and select Create Model
  • Choose Create Model from Data
  • Select Microsoft Azure — you can filter the connection list by Data Storage "Cloud" to find it quickly
  • Choose your connection and select a view
  • The model is created along the structure of the source view, with a live connection established immediately — visible in the data preview

From here, you work with the model as you would with any other SAC planning model. You can remove dimensions and measures you do not need, define calculations directly on the live data, and use data actions to copy actuals from Azure into a plan version stored in SAC's own fact table.

Working with the model

 

Use live version data in planning

Adding a Live Version to an Existing Model

Open your model and navigate to the Data section in the model details. You will find a new entry called External Live Version Data Sources.

From there, the steps are straightforward. The video example is based on Google BigQuery. It is the same workflow, just choose Azure instead.

  • Select Connect External Data Source
  • Choose an existing target version or create a blank one
  • Select Microsoft Azure as the source system
  • Choose your connection
  • Select your data source from Azure
  • Map the data source columns to your model columns — not every source or target column needs to be mapped, measures can be NULL, and dimensions can receive a default member
  • Review any performance hints or master data mismatch notifications — missing dimension members can be added directly from this dialog
  • Start using the live data in calculations, data actions, and stories

Add a live version to an existing model

You can add multiple live sources to a single model if needed.

 

FAQ

Where can I use this feature?

This feature is available for SAP Analytics Cloud models in SAP Analytics Cloud tenants running on HANA Cloud that are not deployed to SAP Datasphere (i.e. not using seamless planning).

Can I use any source view from Azure?

Yes, with the caveat that not all data types are supported. Please refer to the product documentation for the full list of supported data types.

What can I do with live versions — and what are the limitations?

You can:

  • Display live version data in the model data foundation and in tables, charts, and other widgets in stories
  • Reference live version data in model and story calculations as well as data actions, including advanced formulas

You cannot:

  • Write back to the Azure source directly. To persist planned data, copy the live version data into a planning version via copy/paste in the table, data actions, or version management — the plan data is then stored in the SAC fact table

How do I know if data is live or replicated?

In the model data preview, live versions are highlighted and the data source is shown per version in the version dimension. In stories, live version data is locked for data entry and formatted distinctly. Unplannable data reasons provide accurate context.

Conclusion

With Microsoft Azure joining Google BigQuery and Snowflake as a supported live source, SAP Analytics Cloud continues to expand its openness to the data platforms customers already use. Whether you are running your fact data in Azure, Snowflake, or BigQuery, you can now bring it directly into your planning model without any replication overhead — and without giving up the full power of SAC planning, calculations, and stories.

This is part of SAP Analytics Cloud's broader alignment with SAP Business Data Cloud's ambition to be a truly open platform. Follow the Roadmap Explorer to stay up to date on further additions to the live connectivity portfolio. Live connectivity to Microsoft SharePoint Excel files and Microsoft SharePoint Lists is currently planned for QRC4.2026!

Have questions or feedback? Drop them in the comments below.

entry.title: Driving the Future of Water: SAP Brings Together Water Industry Leaders in Madrid
entry.content:
The water sector is at a transformative crossroad. Utilities and public service organizations worldwide are navigating increasing pressure to modernize operations, strengthen resilience, and deliver smarter, more sustainable services to communities. To meet this moment, SAP is organizing the Executive Value Network (EVN) for Water & Wastewater, next October 21-22 in Madrid as an international knowledge sharing oriented forum for those interested in digital transformation best practices of Water and Wastewater companies.

An Event Built on Strong Partnerships

Hosted by Canal de Isabel II, the Water Company of Madrid  and one of Europe’s most recognized water utilities, it will provide the ideal setting for a gathering of this significance. Farys, the Gent based Belgian water, sewage and municipal services company, will as a strategic advisor, contributing deep sector expertise and a collaborative vision for the future.

Complementing a strong ecosystem of support, Minsait and SEW – Smart Energy and Water join as partners, bringing complementary capabilities in digital transformation, technology integration, and water sector innovation. Together, this network of hosts and partners reflects the very spirit of the event: that progress in the water industry is best achieved through collaboration.

Two Keynotes Not to Be Missed: Canal de Isabel II and ANDA

At the heart of the event program will be two exceptional keynote sessions, each telling a compelling and distinct story about digital transformation in the water sector across very different contexts. Together, they demonstrate that while every utility’s journey is unique, the common purpose is universal: delivering better, more sustainable water services to people and communities.

Representing the host utility, María González Cano from Canal de Isabel II will open with a keynote that offers a mature, large-scale example of how to embed digital capabilities into the very heart of water operations and governance. As one of Europe’s leading metropolitan water utilities, Canal de Isabel II’s keynote sets the tone for the entire event — illustrating what is possible when digital ambition meets operational discipline.

Dagoberto Arévalo, CEO, and Dennis Barrientos, CIO of the Administración Nacional de Acueductos y Alcantarillados (ANDA) from El Salvador will bring a different, but equally powerful perspective to the stage. As a national water authority in an emerging economy, ANDA’s Digital Transformation Journey story is one of determination, pragmatism, and impact — demonstrating that digital transformation is a global imperative, not the exclusive domain of large European utilities by executing their 2025-2030 strategic plan.

 

A Rich Agenda: Three Focused Tracks for Every Stage of the Journey

Beyond the keynotes, the event agenda is structured around three main tracks, designed to help participants move from strategic vision to concrete action, regardless of where they are on their digital journey.

 

Strategic Asset Management: From a Technical Approach to a Citizen-Centric Perspective

Sessions will explore how to build a single source of truth for assets by bringing together technical, financial, operational, and service data; how to apply predictive analytics and risk-based planning to prioritize investments where they matter most for customers and communities; and how to connect asset strategies to resilience, sustainability, and quality of service.

The key shift this track champions is simple but powerful: start from citizens’ needs and work backwards into technology and operations — not the other way around.

This track will include an inspirational session which tackles one of the most fundamental questions in water management: how do you plan and invest in infrastructure so that real people receive reliable, safe, and affordable water services?

Maria Gonzalez (Canal Isabel II) and Inge Opreel (Farys)

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Smart Metering

The smart metering track examines how digital measurement and connectivity can transform both operations and customer experience. Topics include architectures for Advanced Metering Infrastructure (AMI) and their integration with SAP back-end systems, turning meter data into actionable insights for leak detection and demand forecasting, enabling new tariff models based on high-frequency consumption data, and managing organizational change as billing, field operations, and customer care become increasingly data-centric.

Sovereign Cloud Strategies

This track addresses one of the most strategic questions facing utilities today: how to embrace cloud innovation while ensuring data protection, compliance, and control. Sessions will cover the concept of sovereign cloud and why it matters for public and critical infrastructure operators, SAP’s approach to supporting data residency, security, and regulatory requirements, and hybrid migration strategies that allow utilities to innovate without disrupting mission-critical operations.

 

Research and Innovation: A Window into What Comes Next

Complementing the three main tracks, the agenda also features a dedicated Research and Innovation stream — designed for those who want to look beyond today’s best practices and anticipate tomorrow’s challenges.

This stream will showcase R&D projects, pilots, and collaborations between utilities, universities, startups, and technology partners. Topics will include AI and machine learning for water operations, digital twins, real-time water quality monitoring, and circular economy initiatives. It is a unique opportunity to discover new ideas and connect with innovators who are already shaping the next generation of water management

 

Networking, Dinner, and Hands-On Experiences

Great events are not only built in the conference room. This program has been designed to foster informal exchange, real-world learning, and lasting connections beyond the formal sessions.

Joint Dinner Sponsored by Minsait: On the evening of the first day, Minsait will host a joint dinner for all participants — a chance to continue the day’s conversations in a more relaxed and convivial atmosphere, explore potential collaborations, and build the kind of relationships that make a real difference back home.

Site Visit on Day Two: Control Center (CPC) and Metrology Laboratory: The second day features an exclusive site visit organized by Canal de Isabel II, offering participants a behind-the-scenes look at how digital strategies translate into real operations, this is connecting what is discussed in the room with what is delivered in the field.

  • Control Center (CPC): See how the Madrid water system is monitored and managed in real time — including how operational data, alarms, and analytics support decision-making and incident response across one of Europe’s largest urban water networks.
  • Metrology Laboratory: Discover how metering devices are tested, calibrated, and certified, and how robust metrology practices underpin reliable billing, smart metering projects, and overall data quality.

 

 

 

 

 

 



Whether you are a utility executive, a digital transformation leader, or a technology innovator, this event offers a unique opportunity to connect with peers, learn from world-class organizations like Canal de Isabel II and ANDA, and explore how SAP’s solutions can help your organization take its next step forward.

We look forward to welcoming you to Madrid.

To register or learn more, visit: https://events.sap.com/evn-water2026/en_us/home.html

entry.title: Handling Blank Values in BW Data Blending and Time Dimension Filters in SAP Analytics Cloud
entry.content:
Introduction

While working with blended SAP BW data in SAP Analytics Cloud, I encountered an issue where some costs were missing from reports. After investigation, the root cause was related to blank date values and the way SAP Analytics Cloud handles time dimension filters.

Issue Description

When blending BW data in SAP Analytics Cloud, blank values are displayed as "#" in reports.

If a dimension filter uses the "Filter by Members" option, there is no data quality issue because blank members are included and displayed as "#".

However, the issue occurs when a dimension filter uses the "Filter by Range" option. A common example is displaying a date dimension at Year level to avoid showing the full date format (DD.MM.YYYY).

In this scenario, SAP Analytics Cloud ignores records with blank date values. As a result, those records are excluded from the report, leading to missing costs and incomplete data.

Impact

The report may appear correct at first glance, but costs associated with records containing blank dates are not included.

This can lead to:

• Missing cost values

• Incomplete reporting

• Potential data quality concerns

Solution

A practical workaround is to use the "Filter by Members" option together with the "Year" hierarchy.

This approach:

• Preserves records with blank dates

• Prevents missing costs

• Maintains reporting at the Year level

One limitation is that blank date records cannot be identified directly through the Year hierarchy.

To identify records with blank dates, apply a filter on another CRM dimension and select "#" (blank value).

Conclusion

When using blended BW data in SAP Analytics Cloud, special attention should be given to time dimension filters. Range-based filtering can unintentionally exclude records with blank dates, resulting in incomplete reporting. Using "Filter by Members" with the Year hierarchy provides a simple and effective workaround while maintaining data completeness.

entry.title: Improving SAP Analytics Cloud Story Performance with a Simple Landing Page
entry.content:
Introduction

Performance issues can negatively impact user experience in SAP Analytics Cloud, especially when stories load large volumes of data immediately upon opening.

In one of our reporting stories, users frequently experienced long loading times, system hanging, and occasional crashes. A simple design change significantly improved the user experience.

Issue

The story contained multiple tables and charts that loaded data at the all products level as soon as the story opened.

As a result:

• Large amounts of data were retrieved immediately.

• Story opening time increased significantly.

• Users experienced system hanging and slow response.

• In some cases, the story became unresponsive.

Solution

Instead of opening the story directly on a data-intensive page, I created a blank landing page without any tables, charts, or other data-consuming widgets.

The landing page contains only filter controls and an instruction message for users.

Message Displayed to Users

"Welcome to the story.

This first page is kept intentionally blank to improve performance and avoid heavy data loading.

Please select and update all required filters first.

After setting the filters, go to the next page to view the data.

This helps reduce loading time and avoids system hanging or crashes."

Benefits

The solution provided several benefits:

• Reduced initial data loading.

• Faster story opening time.

• Lower system resource consumption.

• Improved user experience.

• Reduced complaints about hanging and crashes.

• Increased user satisfaction.

Why This Works

When users first apply the required filters, the amount of data retrieved on subsequent pages is significantly reduced.

Instead of loading all available data at story startup, the system loads only the filtered data required by the user.

This simple approach improves performance without modifying BW queries, models, or backend systems.

Conclusion

Performance improvements do not always require complex technical changes. Sometimes a simple design enhancement can make a significant difference.

Creating a lightweight landing page that encourages users to set filters before viewing data helped reduce loading issues and improved overall user satisfaction in SAP Analytics Cloud.
